manual_dives.title: Natural Language → 3D Scenes: The Pipeline That Didn't Exist 6 Months Ago
manual_dives.thesis: MCP + Blender + Tripo + Claude Code together create a natural language 3D scene builder — describe what you want, get a full 3D scene. This was impossible before MCP existed.

connection_map.DHoPsMOxIRl: Blender MCP + Tripo: the 2D→3D bridge via natural language
connection_map.DIcw6N6snq1: Hunyuan3D-2: free image-to-3D mesh generation (the geometry engine)
connection_map.DXKFrnQEfEQ: Claude Code + Higgsfield: automated motion design + 3D content
connection_map.DFaChZeMdn1: TouchDesigner + MediaPipe: real-time body→3D interaction layer

manual_dives.action_sketch: Set up Blender MCP server → connect to Claude Code → use Tripo/Hunyuan3D for mesh gen → describe scenes in plain English → get rigged, lit 3D environments. Add TouchDesigner for live interaction.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Six months ago, creating a 3D scene meant hours in Blender learning hotkeys, topology, and lighting. Now you type 'a cozy cabin in snow with warm interior lighting' and MCP bridges Claude Code to Blender, Tripo generates the meshes, and Hunyuan3D handles the geometry. The Model Context Protocol is the glue — it lets Claude manipulate Blender like a human operator, but faster. This isn't just automation; it's a new creative medium where language IS the modeling tool. The barrier to 3D went from years of training to a well-written sentence.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Blender (free), Claude Code subscription, Tripo API key (free tier available), MCP server setup (~15 min)

manual_dives.title: The Autonomous Brand Machine: Claude Code as Your Entire Marketing Team
manual_dives.thesis: Claude Code + Playwright MCP + Higgsfield + knowledge graphs = a system that generates ads, UGC, 3D content, and posts it — autonomously. One person runs what used to need a 5-person team.

connection_map.DXKFrnQEfEQ: Claude Code + Higgsfield = automated motion design + ad creation
connection_map.DW4OC7qDj_w: Graphify knowledge graph: Claude maps your entire brand's assets
connection_map.DW2J0eUDKHM: Auto-approve mode: Claude runs the full pipeline unattended
connection_map.DVfZdeqDvyl: iOS Shortcuts integration: trigger campaigns from your phone
connection_map.DWo1P-KDpMf: Obsidian knowledge system: the business brain that feeds the machine

manual_dives.action_sketch: Build Obsidian knowledge base of brand assets → connect via Graphify → Claude Code generates ads/UGC via Higgsfield → Playwright MCP posts to social → iOS Shortcuts for mobile triggers. Total cost: Claude subscription.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: The traditional marketing team — copywriter, designer, videographer, social media manager, strategist — costs $30K+/month. This pipeline replaces all five roles with one Claude Code session that runs autonomously. Playwright MCP handles posting, Higgsfield generates motion content, the Obsidian knowledge graph ensures brand consistency across everything generated. The key insight: auto-approve mode means Claude doesn't stop to ask permission. You set the strategy, the machine executes 24/7. One person with this stack produces more content than a 5-person team, and it gets smarter each cycle because CLAUDE.md captures what works.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Claude Code, Playwright MCP, Higgsfield account, Obsidian vault, social media accounts with API access

manual_dives.title: Physics Models → Market Alpha: When Scientists Become Traders
manual_dives.thesis: 26 posts map physics/math models to trading — avalanche theory, Ising models, phase space reconstruction. The meta-insight: the quant edge isn't in finance, it's in importing models from other fields.

connection_map.DTLkCVXE7O6: Phase Space Reconstruction: topology from physics → BTC volatility
connection_map.DTx3Bi3E-hp: Avalanche Model: earthquake science → market crash prediction
connection_map.DTsn_IKE06E: Ising Model: magnetism → herd behavior modeling
connection_map.DW6uAQdk1r0: Heston Model: stochastic vol → realistic options pricing
connection_map.DU8m2RCE1Ea: Omori's Law: seismology aftershock math → post-crash dynamics
connection_map.DUx9t_FE0CY: Wasserstein Distance: optimal transport → backtest-live gap
connection_map.DT-rdimDamA: Gold Quant Agent: 6M data points + XGBoost = AI trading system
connection_map.DVi7KgKjZbM: Monte Carlo: simulation proves the math works

manual_dives.action_sketch: Study the cross-disciplinary models. Build a signal ensemble that combines 3+ physics-derived indicators. Backtest with Monte Carlo validation. Deploy as an AI agent that runs daily.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Wall Street's edge has always come from importing ideas other traders haven't seen. Renaissance Technologies hired physicists, not MBAs. This catalog maps the same playbook: Ising models from magnetism predict herd behavior, Omori's earthquake law models post-crash dynamics, phase space reconstruction from topology reveals hidden BTC patterns. The meta-insight isn't any single model — it's that the best alpha comes from fields nobody on Wall Street is reading. Each physics paper is a potential trading signal that quant Twitter hasn't priced in yet. The 26 posts here form a complete curriculum for building a cross-disciplinary signal ensemble.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Python + NumPy/SciPy, basic statistics, willingness to read physics papers, backtesting framework (backtrader or custom)

manual_dives.title: The Self-Improving AI Workspace: Claude Code That Gets Smarter Every Session
manual_dives.thesis: CLAUDE.md + primer.md + memory hooks + Obsidian + parallel worktrees = an AI dev environment that learns your preferences, remembers context, and runs multiple streams simultaneously.

connection_map.DV6QY8QjoRW: CLAUDE.md as living rulebook: self-improving instructions
connection_map.DVQ5o6YDfsp: Parallel worktrees: 10-15 Claude sessions running simultaneously
connection_map.DVzvsgijOLT: primer.md: live session state that persists across context resets
connection_map.DXCu2p4EQe3: Obsidian second brain: 3 systems you need for persistent AI memory
connection_map.DWo1P-KDpMf: 3D knowledge graph: your entire business spinning in real time
connection_map.DWoxW7pjjkH: Claude as tutor: PDF guides generated from your knowledge base

manual_dives.action_sketch: Set up CLAUDE.md with self-improvement rules → add primer.md for session continuity → connect Obsidian vault → enable parallel worktrees for concurrent work → watch the system compound over weeks.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Most people use Claude as a chatbot — ask a question, get an answer, context dies. This stack makes Claude a persistent collaborator that remembers your preferences, coding style, and project history across sessions. CLAUDE.md is the living rulebook (it self-improves when you correct mistakes), primer.md carries session state across context resets, parallel worktrees let you run 10-15 Claude sessions simultaneously on different parts of the same codebase. The compounding effect is real: after a week, Claude knows your patterns better than a new hire would after a month. After a month, it's like having a senior engineer who's read every line of your code.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Claude Code subscription, git (for worktrees), Obsidian (optional but recommended), ~30 min initial setup

manual_dives.title: Napkin Sketch → Production Asset: The 5-Tool Creative Pipeline
manual_dives.thesis: A hand-drawn sketch can become a production-ready 3D animation in under an hour by chaining Procreate → Magnific AI → 3DAI Studio → Move AI → final render. Each tool handles one transformation step.

connection_map.C5010-Kh1Mv: The full pipeline: sketch → upscale → 3D → motion capture → animate
connection_map.DDFdSEDtKZV: Kling Motion Brush: direct up to 5 characters in one AI video
connection_map.DIcw6N6snq1: Hunyuan3D-2: skip the 3D modeling step entirely with AI
connection_map.DHTvblkRgZG: AI rotoscoping: mask yourself into your own animations

manual_dives.action_sketch: Draw in Procreate → upscale with Magnific → generate 3D mesh with Hunyuan3D or 3DAI → capture motion with Move AI → composite with Kling or Runway. Total time: ~45 minutes per asset.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: The gap between 'I have an idea' and 'I have a production asset' used to be weeks of specialized work across multiple disciplines. This pipeline collapses it to under an hour by chaining five AI tools, each handling one transformation: sketch → upscale → 3D mesh → motion → final render. The key is that each tool is best-in-class at its specific step, and they hand off cleanly. A napkin doodle at lunch becomes a rigged, animated 3D character by dinner. For indie creators, this is the difference between having ideas and shipping them.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Procreate or any drawing app, Magnific AI, Hunyuan3D-2 or 3DAI Studio, Kling or Runway account

manual_dives.title: Body → Machine → Art: Real-Time Interactive Installations Anyone Can Build
manual_dives.thesis: Kinect/MediaPipe body tracking + TouchDesigner + Unreal Engine = interactive art installations that respond to human movement. Previously needed a studio budget; now runs on a gaming laptop.

connection_map.DVoReJNCGxT: DATA CONTROL: Kinect + TouchDesigner + Unreal for scientific viz
connection_map.DFaChZeMdn1: MediaPipe + TouchDesigner: body tracking without expensive hardware
connection_map.DW9OgzCFcGY: Mirror systems: engineering meets art in mechanical installations
connection_map.CvsDXJbJh6O: Cymatics: sound → physical form → visual art
connection_map.CwNnre_Ih9l: Al Bahar Towers: architecture that responds to sunlight

manual_dives.action_sketch: MediaPipe for body tracking (free, runs on webcam) → pipe data to TouchDesigner → drive visuals in Unreal or project with a $200 projector. Gallery-quality interactive art for under $500 in hardware.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Interactive art installations used to require a $50K+ budget: custom hardware, specialized software, a team of creative technologists. Now MediaPipe runs body tracking on a $30 webcam, TouchDesigner handles the visual pipeline, and a $200 projector fills a wall. The total cost dropped 99%. This democratization means independent artists can build gallery-quality interactive work in their apartment. The posts here map the complete stack from body input to visual output, including real examples of installations that respond to movement, sound, and light.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Webcam, TouchDesigner (free for non-commercial), projector ($200+), laptop with decent GPU

manual_dives.title: The One-Person Video Studio: AI Tools That Replace a Production Team
manual_dives.thesis: Runway + Kling + rotoscoping + motion brush = professional video production without a crew, studio, or actors. Direct scenes with text prompts and motion controls.

connection_map.DDIAME6MhzY: Runway node canvas: non-linear AI video editing
connection_map.DDFdSEDtKZV: Kling Motion Brush: direct character movement in AI video
connection_map.C_8ia4lsMB3: Runway Gen-3: precise video-to-video motion control
connection_map.C91jKVfxtG1: Kling free rollout: no more waitlists, everyone has access
connection_map.DHTvblkRgZG: AI rotoscoping: insert yourself into any scene

manual_dives.action_sketch: Script with Claude → generate base footage with Runway/Kling → direct characters with motion brush → rotoscope yourself in → edit on the node canvas → export. Cost: ~$30/month in AI tools.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Professional video production has three bottlenecks: talent (actors/crew), equipment (cameras/lighting/studio), and post-production (editing/VFX). AI tools collapse all three. Runway's node canvas replaces the editing suite. Kling's motion brush replaces the director ('move this character's arm here'). Rotoscoping lets you insert yourself into any scene without a green screen. OpenScreen handles screen recording for tutorials. The result: one person with a laptop produces content that previously needed a production team. The cost dropped from $10K/video to $30/month in subscriptions.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Runway account (~$12/mo), Kling (free tier), OpenScreen (free), basic video editing concept

manual_dives.title: Code as Canvas: When Algorithms Become the Artist
manual_dives.thesis: Ant colonies, boid simulations, particle systems, and generative algorithms create art that's alive — emergent beauty from simple rules. The artist writes the rules, not the pixels.

connection_map.DVCBNQ8jCly: Ant Colony Optimization: pheromone trails become visual patterns
connection_map.DVndfhOE7A9: Boid flocking: emergent crowd behavior as living art
connection_map.DWLMdCUN1eR: LLM canvas coding: plant-creature prompts as creative constraint
connection_map.DWlrC88Db2r: Mix Universe: accidental beauty from music + code

manual_dives.action_sketch: Pick a natural system (flocking, diffusion, growth). Code it in p5.js or TouchDesigner. Add interaction (sound, body tracking, data feeds). The art emerges — you don't draw it, you evolve it.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Traditional art is about control — every brushstroke is intentional. Generative art inverts this: you write rules, and beauty emerges from complexity. Ant colony simulations create organic trail patterns. Boid flocking algorithms produce mesmerizing crowd dynamics. Particle systems generate physics-driven visual poetry. The artist's role shifts from pixel-pusher to system designer. The posts here show the full spectrum: from mathematical simulations to creative coding gardens to accidental beauty from music+code. This is art that's alive — it never produces the same output twice.
manual_dives.prerequisites: p5.js or Processing (both free), basic programming concepts, optional: TouchDesigner for real-time interaction

manual_dives.title: Why Your Agent Sucks: Architecture Patterns That Actually Work
manual_dives.thesis: Better models won't fix broken agent architecture. The gap between demo agents and production agents is context management, memory hierarchy, and harness engineering — not model capability.

connection_map.DXStFLkkphz: The thesis: model upgrades mask architecture debt. Fix the scaffold, not the brain.
connection_map.DXx6KhJshmv: Three disciplines — context, memory, harness — that separate toy agents from real ones
connection_map.DXYSxNLpIAY: Building an agentic OS: the orchestration layer above individual agents
connection_map.DWtgbzLDgwm: Onyx: production-grade self-hosted RAG — the retrieval layer agents actually need
connection_map.DXKAbR8ETEo: Giving agents perception: MCP as the sensory bridge to external tools
connection_map.MoonshotAI/Kimi-K2.5: Kimi K2.5: open multimodal agentic model — when the model IS the agent

manual_dives.action_sketch: Audit your agent's context window usage → separate context (ephemeral) from memory (persistent) from harness (structural) → implement retrieval layer (Onyx or similar) → add tool-use via MCP → test with Claude Code as the orchestrator → measure task completion, not conversation quality.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Everyone's building agents. Almost nobody's building agents that work past the demo. The 103 new entries from this session surfaced a pattern: the posts that actually teach agent building focus on architecture — context management, memory hierarchies, harness engineering — not on which model to use. The context vs memory vs harness framework is the clearest mental model we've seen for why most agents fail: they dump everything into context, have no persistent memory, and lack structural guardrails. This dive connects the theory (architecture patterns) with the infrastructure (Onyx, MCP, Kimi) needed to implement it.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Familiarity with LLM APIs, basic understanding of RAG. Claude Code or similar agent framework.

manual_dives.title: 25 Agents Walk Into a Market: AI-Powered Trading Infrastructure
manual_dives.thesis: Quantitative finance is being rebuilt on AI agents — not as black-box predictors, but as multi-agent debate systems that combine physics models, regime detection, and autonomous execution.

connection_map.DX9aDFDOMS1: The architecture: 25+ agents debating across 4 layers, scored on real outcomes
connection_map.DW1kTMcExSj: Hidden Markov Models: the regime detection layer — know which market you're in
connection_map.DW6uAQdk1r0: Heston Model: stochastic volatility for realistic options pricing
connection_map.DTLkCVXE7O6: Phase Space Reconstruction: unfolding hidden dynamics from price series alone
connection_map.DWzAGZzE9gV: Avellaneda-Stoikov: optimal spread calculation — the execution engine
connection_map.DW4GU9HE8nd: Cox Process: modeling the bursts and clusters in market events

manual_dives.action_sketch: Build regime detection with HMMs → layer Heston vol surface for options → add phase space reconstruction for hidden dynamics → implement A-S market making for execution → wrap in multi-agent debate system where agents specialize by model type → score on real P&L, not backtest metrics.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: The stock2 collection revealed something the quant collection hinted at: the next generation of trading systems isn't a single model predicting price direction. It's a parliament of specialized agents — one running HMMs for regime detection, one computing Heston vol surfaces, one monitoring Cox process bursts — that debate and vote before any trade executes. The 25-agent debate system entry is the architecture pattern; the quant physics models are the individual specialist brains. Together they form a trading infrastructure where agents with different world-models challenge each other's assumptions in real time.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Python, basic stochastic calculus concepts, market data API access. Familiarity with at least one quant model.

manual_dives.title: The Claude Code Power User's Toolkit: From Token Waste to Terminal Dominance

connection_map.DXStFLkkphz: Architecture insight: better models won't fix your workflow — configuration will

manual_dives.action_sketch: Write a CLAUDE.md with project rules + persona → add primer.md for session state → install hooks for automation → set up parallel worktrees for multi-agent work → install the 7 key repos → configure memory system → measure token usage and optimize.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Most Claude Code users treat it as a fancy autocomplete. The Boris Cherny posts revealed the real architecture: CLAUDE.md as persistent instruction set, primer.md as session memory, hooks as automation triggers, and parallel worktrees for concurrent agents. Combined with the 'burning tokens is a skill issue' insight from ai6, this forms a complete toolkit for going from 'talking to a chatbot' to 'managing an autonomous engineering team.' The gap between casual and power user is entirely configuration — no new tools needed, just better use of what's already there.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Claude Code subscription, git (for worktrees), 30 minutes to configure CLAUDE.md and primer.md

manual_dives.title: Prompt Engineering Isn't Dead — It Just Grew Up
manual_dives.thesis: Prompt engineering evolved from 'add please' to a real discipline: structured frameworks, adversarial testing, llms.txt standards, and system-level prompt architecture that shapes model behavior at scale.

connection_map.DWoYMJDD6oO: Anthropic's own system prompt: how the best in the business structure model instructions
connection_map.DWxmSwTDFk2: llms.txt: the emerging standard for making your content LLM-discoverable
connection_map.DOPqBfyDMHG: L1B3RT4S: adversarial prompt testing — understanding prompt security by breaking it
connection_map.DIe7QoQCong: Google's framework: the corporate playbook for structured prompt engineering
connection_map.DWoZspWDo6F: GitReverse: reverse-engineering prompts from outputs — the analytical approach
connection_map.DU5Wjs2DbWS: McKinsey-style research prompts: domain-specific prompt templates at professional scale

manual_dives.action_sketch: Study Anthropic's leaked system prompt for structure patterns → implement llms.txt for your own sites → build a prompt library organized by use case (research, code, creative) → test prompts adversarially with L1B3RT4S techniques → add GitReverse to your workflow for learning from others' prompts.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: The 'prompt engineering is dead' crowd missed the plot. It didn't die — it professionalized. Anthropic's own leaked system prompt shows structured instruction architecture. llms.txt is becoming a web standard. Google published a formal framework. The L1B3RT4S jailbreaks prove that prompt security is a real engineering discipline. And GitReverse shows you can reverse-engineer any output back to its prompt. This isn't 'ask nicely' anymore — it's systems engineering for language models.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Any LLM API access. Basic understanding of system vs user prompts.

manual_dives.title: The Incentive Trap: Game Theory for the AI Age
manual_dives.thesis: Game theory isn't abstract math — it's the operating system behind every AI product decision, every market move, and every platform play. The entries in this collection reveal the incentive structures hiding in plain sight.

connection_map.DXprHQ8Dg3G: The incentive trap: how $1 commitments create billion-dollar lock-in
connection_map.DXiR_RyCF8s: Failed strategies: what happens when you ignore the game-theoretic structure
connection_map.DV6c9ItjPZp: AI's origin game: decades of academic incentives that produced the current landscape
connection_map.DRzrIiDDjN5: Physics as game theory: everything reduces to forces and equilibria
connection_map.DXMt7CtEeEo: Optimization puzzles: the mathematical backbone of strategic thinking
connection_map.DXcs7NJiSy1: Radiation patterns: physical systems as strategic actors

manual_dives.action_sketch: Map the incentive structure before making any decision → identify who benefits from each outcome → look for lock-in mechanisms (Elon's $1 pattern) → study failed strategies to understand what the 'game' actually is → apply physics intuition (equilibria, forces) to strategic situations.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: The game-theory collection is new and small but punches above its weight. Elon's $1 is a masterclass in incentive design — the trap is always in the commitment structure, not the headline number. Combined with the AI origin story (decades of academic game theory producing today's landscape) and the physics-as-strategy entries, this collection reveals that every major decision has a game-theoretic structure. Understanding it is the difference between being a player and being played.
manual_dives.prerequisites: None — these are mental models, not technical implementations.

manual_dives.title: The One-Person Creative Empire: AI Tools That Replace an Entire Agency

manual_dives.action_sketch: Set up Claude Code as creative orchestrator → connect Figma for design → add Runway/Higgsfield for video → TouchDesigner + MediaPipe for interactive → Cursor for web builds → run marketing, design, video, and interactive art from a single workstation.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: The creative collection tells a single story: the minimum viable creative agency is now one person with the right AI tools. Not in a 'kinda works if you squint' way — in a 'produced a full marketing campaign, interactive art installation, and production website in a weekend' way. Claude Code isn't just a coding tool here; it's the orchestration layer that connects Figma (design), Runway/Higgsfield (video), TouchDesigner (interactive), and React (web) into a unified creative pipeline. The creative middle class — people with taste but not a team — now has a studio.
manual_dives.prerequisites: Claude Code, Figma (free tier), TouchDesigner (free), basic comfort with terminal. Budget for Runway/Higgsfield optional.

manual_dives.title: The Claude Harness Pattern: Context, Memory, and Cognitive Scaffolding
manual_dives.thesis: The most effective Claude users aren't writing better prompts — they're building harnesses: layered systems of CLAUDE.md files, skills, hooks, and memory that turn a chat window into a persistent, self-improving agent. This pattern emerged independently across multiple creators and maps directly to how production AI systems are being architected.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: The gap between 'using Claude' and 'building with Claude' is exactly this pattern. Context engineering (what goes in the window), memory (what persists across sessions), and harness (the scaffold that orchestrates both) are three distinct disciplines. Most people conflate them. The entries in this dive show practitioners who figured out the distinction independently.
prerequisites: Claude Code or similar agent-mode tool
manual_dives.action_sketch:
1. Audit your current Claude setup — do you have a CLAUDE.md? Skills? Hooks?
2. Map your workflow: what context does Claude need every session vs. occasionally?
3. Build a minimal harness: CLAUDE.md (permanent rules) + primer.md (session state) + skills/ (reusable workflows)
4. Add memory persistence: file-based or MCP-backed cross-session recall
5. Layer in hooks for self-correction (caveman mode, token discipline, audit-after-step)

manual_dives.title: MediaPipe × TouchDesigner: Real-Time Body-to-Art Pipeline
manual_dives.thesis: MediaPipe's body/hand/face tracking combined with TouchDesigner's node-based visual programming creates a real-time pipeline where human movement becomes generative art. Multiple creators are independently converging on this stack — from spider-web string simulations to 3D Gaussian splatting to interactive music visualizers.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: This is the democratization of interactive installation art. What used to require a team, custom hardware, and months of C++ now runs on a laptop with a webcam. The convergence of multiple creators on this exact stack signals it's become the de facto standard for real-time body-reactive art.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of node-based visual programming
manual_dives.action_sketch:
1. Install TouchDesigner + MediaPipe Python bindings
2. Set up basic hand landmark tracking (21 points per hand)
3. Pipe landmark data into TouchDesigner via OSC or shared memory
4. Build a simple particle system that responds to hand position
5. Layer in face mesh (468 landmarks) for expression-reactive visuals
6. Export as standalone for installations or stream via NDI

manual_dives.title: Beyond Prompting: Prompt Architecture as Engineering Discipline
manual_dives.thesis: Prompt engineering is dead. What's replacing it is prompt architecture — the systematic design of context windows, memory layers, retrieval systems, and cognitive scaffolds. The shift from 'write a better prompt' to 'build a better system around the model' is the biggest unlock in practical AI right now.

manual_dives.why_it_matters: Most people are still optimizing individual prompts. The practitioners in these entries have moved past that entirely — they're building systems where the prompt is just one component of a larger architecture. Context management, memory persistence, tool orchestration, and self-correction loops matter more than any single prompt.
prerequisites: Experience with at least one LLM API
manual_dives.action_sketch:
1. Stop optimizing prompts in isolation — map the full context flow
2. Implement RAG with hybrid search + reranking for internal docs
3. Build a memory layer: what the model needs to remember across sessions
4. Add retrieval-augmented generation for domain-specific knowledge
5. Design self-correction: let the model audit its own output
6. Measure: track which context components actually improve output quality

manual_dives.title: Art Without Gatekeepers: How AI is Rebuilding the Creative Economy
manual_dives.thesis: The art market's gatekeeping structure — galleries, curators, collectors, degree requirements — is being dismantled by AI tools and direct distribution. Artists who adapt are finding new revenue streams (AI-assisted commissions, generative art NFTs, interactive installations). Those who resist are watching their distribution channels commoditize around them.

manual_dives.why_it_matters: For decades, art distribution required permission — from galleries, from collectors, from institutions. AI tools + direct platforms are removing that dependency. The entries show both the opportunity (new tools, new markets, new forms) and the risk (commoditization of traditional skills).
prerequisites: Existing art practice or strong visual sense
Willingness to experiment with AI tools
manual_dives.action_sketch:
1. Inventory your skills: what do AI tools amplify vs. replace?
2. Experiment with AI-assisted workflow (not AI-generated — AI-assisted)
3. Build direct distribution: portfolio site, social presence, email list
4. Explore interactive/generative formats that galleries can't replicate
5. Price for the new market: commissions, prints, installations, licensing
6. Document your process — the behind-the-scenes is the new gallery wall

manual_dives.title: Autonomous Agents in the Wild: What's Actually Working in 2026
manual_dives.thesis: Autonomous agents went from demo-ware to daily drivers in 2026. But the gap between 'impressive demo' and 'reliable tool' is massive. These entries map the frontier: Qwen agents roaming NYC streets, Claude automating daily workflows, Kronos failing hedge fund stress tests. The pattern: narrow agents with clear guardrails work; general agents with vague goals don't.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Everyone's building agents. Most are failing. The entries in this dive separate signal from noise: what agent patterns actually work in production, what fails under stress, and where the reliability boundary sits. The common thread: agents succeed when scope is narrow, feedback is fast, and failure is cheap.
prerequisites: Understanding of LLM capabilities and limits
Experience with at least one agent framework
manual_dives.action_sketch:
1. Start narrow: one task, one tool, one feedback loop
2. Build in guardrails before capability (kill switches, budget limits, scope constraints)
3. Test adversarially: what happens when the agent encounters edge cases?
4. Add persistence: agents need memory to avoid repeating mistakes
5. Monitor: log everything, review regularly, prune bad patterns
6. Scale horizontally: multiple narrow agents > one general agent

manual_dives.title: Claude Mastery: From First Install to Advanced Agent Architecture
manual_dives.thesis: Claude Code is not a chatbot — it's a programmable agent framework. The gap between 'using Claude' and 'mastering Claude' is a stack of learnable patterns: CLAUDE.md configuration, skills/hooks/MCP extensibility, memory persistence, cognitive scaffolding, prompt architecture, parallel worktrees, and multi-agent orchestration. This guide maps every layer with dated, sourced entries that track the evolution from Opus 4.6 through Fable 5.
manual_dives.why_it_matters: Most Claude users are leaving 90% of its capability on the table. Skills, hooks, MCP servers, memory systems, and agent orchestration transform a conversational AI into a persistent, self-improving development partner. This is the definitive reference — living document that grows as Claude evolves.
manual_dives.action_sketch:
1. Install Claude Code: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
2. Create CLAUDE.md in your project root with behavioral rules
3. Install 2-3 starter skills (brainstorming, systematic-debugging)
4. Set up auto-memory (file-based or MCP-backed)
5. Add hooks for session-start context loading
6. Learn thinking keywords (think → ultrathink progression)
7. Try parallel worktrees for multi-branch development
8. Build custom skills for your recurring workflows
